notes.anchor: on the sixth day before the Ides
notes.note: Roman dates were counted backwards and inclusively from three fixed points: the Kalends (1st), the Nones (5th or 7th), and the Ides (13th or 15th). The sixth day before the Ides of May here is 10 May 49 BC.

notes.anchor: Eros, the agent of Philotimus, will tell you
notes.note: Eros was a financial accountant who kept the books of Cicero's circle; Philotimus was Terentia's freedman and a busy steward of the family's money. Cicero routinely referred Atticus to such agents for the precise figures of his tangled loans.

notes.anchor: the affair in Caelius's letter, is ripening
notes.note: The "Caelian" matter is some scheme reported by M. Caelius Rufus, here used as a cipher to keep the plan to slip away and join Pompey out of unfriendly hands. Cicero's letters of these weeks are deliberately allusive for fear of interception.

A. Allienus held a praetorship in 49 BC and was being pressed to take up a provincial command for Caesar. Cicero notes that the office could fall to him or to one of his fellow praetors --- "anyone will serve."

notes.anchor: he is in no small trouble over the loan transfer
notes.note: A versura was the raising of a fresh loan to pay off an old one, a common but precarious device in Roman finance. Quintus's struggle to arrange one through Lucius Egnatius reflects the credit squeeze that gripped Italy at the outbreak of civil war.

notes.anchor: I am glad you are free of the quartan
notes.note: The quartan was a recurrent fever (most likely malarial) whose paroxysms returned every fourth day by inclusive Roman reckoning. It was a chronic affliction much remarked on in Cicero's correspondence with the ailing Atticus.
